studies.title: Intermittent theta-burst stimulation for major depressive disorder with comorbid anxiety: a systematic review and meta-analysis
studies.abstract: Systematic review and meta-analysis of iTBS for major depressive disorder with comorbid anxiety disorders. Reviews efficacy and safety of iTBS across studies including MDD with anxiety comorbidity.

studies.title: Intermittent versus continuous theta burst stimulation for major depressive disorder and bipolar depression: a systematic review
studies.abstract: Systematic review comparing intermittent (iTBS) versus continuous theta burst stimulation (cTBS) protocols for major depressive disorder and bipolar depression.

studies.title: Efficacy and safety of accelerated transcranial magnetic stimulation on suicidality in depressive disorders: A systematic review and meta-analysis
studies.authors: Zheng W, Li XB, Cai DB, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: Accelerated TMS (aTMS; ≥2 sessions/day) has been proposed as a rapid-acting intervention for suicidal crisis. We systematically reviewed and meta-analysed its efficacy and safety for suicidality in depressive disorders. Methods: 13 studies (7 RCTs, 6 open-label; N=625 total) were included; 6 RCTs (N=310) contributed to the primary meta-analysis. Databases searched: PubMed, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science through April 30, 2025. Results: aTMS showed no statistically significant advantage over sham or standard TMS for suicidality at post-treatment or follow-up. A non-significant trend favouring aTMS was observed for suicidality response. Open-label studies suggested potential benefits of fMRI-guided individualized aTMS protocols. Adverse effects were mild but more frequent with aTMS than sham. Conclusion: Current evidence does not support a specific anti-suicidal effect of aTMS beyond standard TMS. Larger RCTs with individualized targeting are needed.

studies.title: Theta burst stimulation of temporo-parietal cortex regions for the treatment of persistent auditory hallucinations: a multicentre, randomised, sham-controlled, triple-blind phase 3 trial in Germany
studies.authors: Plewnia C, Schönfeldt-Lecuona C, Goerigk S, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: Persistent auditory hallucinations in schizophrenia spectrum disorders are often resistant to antipsychotic medication. Continuous theta burst stimulation (cTBS) over temporo-parietal cortex regions has shown promise in smaller trials. Methods: In this multicentre, randomised, sham-controlled, triple-blind phase 3 trial (TBS-AH) at 7 German psychiatric university hospitals, 138 patients (70 active, 68 sham) with persistent auditory hallucinations despite adequate antipsychotic treatment received 15 sessions of bilateral cTBS (600 pulses left + 600 pulses right temporo-parietal cortex) over 3 weeks, or matched sham stimulation. Primary outcome was change in PSYRATS-AH score at week 3. Results: PSYRATS-AH scores decreased by mean 6.36 points (SD 7.97) in the active group versus 3.74 points (SD 5.79) in the sham group; adjusted difference −2.36 (95% CI −4.71 to −0.01; p=0.042). Response rates and tolerability were favourable; adverse events were mild and transient. Conclusion: Bilateral cTBS of temporo-parietal cortex significantly reduces persistent auditory hallucination severity in schizophrenia, supporting cTBS as an evidence-based add-on treatment.

studies.title: Comparative Effects of Intermittent Theta-Burst Stimulation and Sequential Bilateral rTMS on Depression and Emotion Regulation in Major Depressive Disorder: A Randomized Controlled Trial
studies.authors: Liu X, Zhang H, Wang Y, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: iTBS (intermittent theta burst stimulation) delivers similar clinical effects to standard high-frequency rTMS in a fraction of the time, but head-to-head comparisons with sequential bilateral rTMS protocols are scarce, especially regarding emotion regulation. Methods: 114 patients with MDD (106 completing) were randomized to sequential bilateral rTMS (10 Hz left DLPFC + 1 Hz right DLPFC; n=50) or iTBS left DLPFC (n=56) for 2 weeks (10 sessions). Primary outcome: HAMD-17 score. Secondary outcomes: cognitive reappraisal (ERQ), anhedonia (SHAPS), anxiety (HAMA). Results: iTBS showed significantly greater reductions in HAMD-17 scores (p<0.05) and significantly greater improvements in cognitive reappraisal compared to sequential bilateral rTMS. Both groups improved in anhedonia and anxiety, with no significant between-group differences for these outcomes. Tolerability was similar in both arms. Conclusion: iTBS appears superior to sequential bilateral rTMS for depression and emotion regulation outcomes in MDD over a 2-week protocol, supporting its preferential use in clinical practice.

studies.title: Optimized multichannel 4 mA vs conventional transcranial direct current stimulation for major depressive disorder: A randomized sham-controlled trial
studies.authors: Nikolin S, Loo CK, Vanderhasselt MA, et al.
studies.abstract: Background: Conventional tDCS (2 mA, 2 electrodes) has modest antidepressant effects. Multichannel HD-tDCS allows focal current delivery at higher intensities (4 mA via 7 electrodes over left DLPFC). Methods: 71 patients with major depressive disorder were randomized to multichannel tDCS 4 mA, conventional tDCS 2 mA, or sham in a three-arm trial. Depression ratings and cognitive control measures were assessed at baseline, mid-treatment, and end of treatment. Results: Multichannel tDCS 4 mA produced earlier and significantly stronger antidepressant effects compared to conventional tDCS and sham. Improvements in cognitive control correlated with antidepressant response. Functional connectivity markers associated with depression pathophysiology were modulated specifically by multichannel tDCS. Conclusion: Optimized multichannel HD-tDCS at 4 mA offers superior antidepressant efficacy over conventional 2 mA tDCS, with a favourable tolerability profile.

studies.title: Home-based transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) for bipolar depression: effects on quality of life and functioning—an open-label study
studies.authors: Rezaei H, Woodham RD, Ghazi-Noori AR, Bramon E, Bauer M, Young AH, Fu CHY, Ritter P
studies.abstract: Background: Bipolar depression causes significant impairment in quality of life (QoL) and psychosocial functioning. Home-based tDCS has shown feasibility for unipolar depression but its impact on QoL in bipolar depression is unknown. Methods: 44 adults (31 women) with bipolar depression of at least moderate severity received 21 sessions of home-based tDCS (2 mA, 30 min/session, F3 anode / F4 cathode) over 6 weeks, with follow-up at 5 months. Primary outcome: Quality of Life Enjoyment and Satisfaction Questionnaire (Q-LES-Q). Results: Q-LES-Q total scores improved significantly at week 6 (p<0.001), with improvements maintained at 5-month follow-up. QoL gains were closely linked to reduction in depressive symptoms. Functioning improved across social and occupational domains. No serious adverse events occurred. Conclusion: Home-based tDCS is associated with clinically meaningful improvements in QoL and functioning in bipolar depression. Open-label design limits causal inference; controlled trial needed.

studies.title: Non-invasive brain stimulation for auditory hallucinations: rTMS, tDCS, and deep brain stimulation — a scoping review
studies.abstract: Scoping review of non-invasive brain stimulation techniques (rTMS, tDCS, DBS) for treatment of auditory hallucinations in psychotic disorders.

studies.title: Home-based accelerated intermittent theta burst stimulation for depression: a case series
studies.abstract: Case series describing home-based accelerated intermittent theta burst stimulation (aiTBS) in patients with treatment-resistant depression.

studies.title: Biomarkers predicting transcranial direct current stimulation response in psychiatric disorders: A scoping review
studies.abstract: Scoping review of biomarkers (neuroimaging, neurophysiological, genetic, inflammatory) used to predict response to transcranial direct current stimulation across psychiatric disorders.

studies.title: Transcranial direct current stimulation in depression: a bibliometric analysis
studies.abstract: Bibliometric analysis of the tDCS literature in depression, mapping publication trends, key authors, journals, and research themes.
